narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 45.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your fourth-decile ranking in Year 1 grades (below the typical student's 5.71 average), a first-decile ranking in Year 3 (well below the 5.53 average), lower-middle family income, an LSAT score of 39 (stronger than the 36.7 average), and an undergraduate GPA of 2.3 (lower than the 3.21 average). You're also a full-time female student identifying as White, which aligns closely with common profiles in our dataset—about 44% female, 84% White, and 93% full-time. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your Year 1 decile ranking, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure compared to stronger early performers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which, despite being above average, still nudged the outlook slightly toward failure in the model's eyes for your specific combination of traits. Your family income level followed closely, contributing to the risk assessment in a similar way. Your Year 3 decile had a notable but slightly lesser impact, reinforcing concerns about later academic performance. Your undergraduate GPA and racial background played supporting roles in elevating the failure probability, while your status as a full-time student had minimal sway. Interestingly, your gender as female was the one factor that gently pulled the prediction toward success, offering a small positive offset. Overall, these academic markers—especially your deciles and GPA lagging behind averages—drove about 80% of the decision's direction, far outweighing demographics. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your grades to climb higher deciles through dedicated study habits or tutoring, as those are areas within your control before reapplying. Strengthening any weak academic spots now could tip future predictions toward bar passage and approval. We believe in your potential, especially with that solid LSAT—keep pushing forward.
